original_hypothesis.title: Lifecycle can be stuck/incorrect due to liveNativeTokens removal while iterating (skipping pools during epoch transitions)
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: checkBeforeEpochFlip and checkAtEpochFlip iterate liveNativeTokens in reverse while _removeLiveToken swaps in the last element. This can cause the swapped-in token to be skipped in the same loop iteration schedule, delaying disqualification/launch transitions for some pools and potentially allowing extra deposits or preventing timely launch.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Phase A flagged a specific skip risk; even with trusted sequencer and no mempool, multi-pool epoch operations are critical and a systematic skip can repeatedly delay state transitions, breaking time-based invariants for genesis pools.

proof_of_signal.lines: checkBeforeEpochFlip(): for(i = length; i > 0; i--) { ... _removeLiveToken(nativeToken); } with swap-pop removal
proof_of_signal.pattern: Modifying array during reverse iteration with swap-and-pop can skip the swapped element

preconditions: At least two liveNativeTokens exist where one is removed during the loop
The swapped-in token also needs processing in the same call (e.g., also eligible for disqualify or needs deposit-disabled transition)
EpochController calls are infrequent enough that a skip causes meaningful delay

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: A pool may miss its intended transition window (e.g., deposits not disabled on time)
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No re-check of current index element after removal; no mark-and-sweep approach

analysis_questions: Can a skipped pool remain eligible but unprocessed beyond its intended window (e.g., last-epoch checks)?
Does the system rely on exact timing (NO_GENESIS_DEPOSIT_WINDOW) such that a one-cycle delay is harmful?
Is there any external keeper guarantee about multiple calls per epoch?
validation_requirements: Unit test with 3+ live tokens where two satisfy removal conditions; assert all intended transitions occur in one call
Simulate repeated epochs to see whether a pool can be perpetually skipped under certain patterns

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: A pool that should be disqualified is swapped into an index already passed; it is not processed until a later call, allowing it to remain in PRE_LISTING longer than intended.
prerequisites: Multiple live pools and at least one removal event in the loop

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Moderate: can allow extra deposits, delay launches, or mis-time disqualifications impacting fairness and pricing
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Depositors across multiple genesis pools in the same epoch window
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Operational workaround by calling upkeep multiple times; code fix preferable

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Missing multi-pool epoch transition tests with removals

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: On-chain observation of pools not transitioning despite eligibility

validation_result.impact_summary: No skip occurs because reverse iteration always visits the last element first; swap-and-pop only moves already-visited elements into already-processed positions.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The hypothesis claims that iterating liveNativeTokens in reverse while using swap-and-pop removal can cause elements to be skipped. This is a well-known concern for FORWARD iteration with swap-and-pop, but it does NOT apply to REVERSE iteration.

In the reverse loop `for(i = _proposedTokensCnt; i > 0; i--)`, elements are processed from the highest index down to 0. When `_removeLiveToken` is called, it swaps the element at the removed position with the LAST element in the array, then pops. Since we iterate backwards, the last element has ALREADY been visited in an earlier iteration (it was at a higher or equal index). Therefore, the swapped-in element is never a new unvisited element.

I traced multiple concrete scenarios with 3-6 elements where various combinations of removals occur (end, middle, consecutive, non-consecutive) and confirmed that every element is visited exactly once. The array length decrements at most as fast as the loop counter `i`, so no out-of-bounds access occurs either.

The invariant is: `array.length >= i` at the start of each iteration, which holds because: (1) initially length == i, and (2) each iteration decreases i by 1, while length decreases by 0 or 1 (at most one removal per iteration). Thus length - i is non-decreasing from its initial value of 0.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The reverse iteration pattern in both checkBeforeEpochFlip (GenesisPoolManager.sol) and checkAtEpochFlip (GenesisPoolManager.sol) is safe. The loop `for(i = _proposedTokensCnt; i > 0; i--)` processes from the end of the array to the beginning. The _removeLiveToken function uses swap-and-pop, bringing the last element to the removed position. Since the last element was already processed (it was at a higher index, visited earlier in the reverse loop), no element is skipped.

calling_sequence: 1. Loop: for(i = _proposedTokensCnt; i > 0; i--) accesses liveNativeTokens[i-1]
3. _removeLiveToken swaps last element to removed position and pops
4. The swapped-in element was originally at a higher index, already visited in an earlier iteration
5. Loop counter i decreases by 1, processing the next lower index
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: No vulnerable path exists. Reverse iteration ensures all elements are visited. Concrete trace: array [A,B,C] with all needing removal: i=3 removes C (last, pop to [A,B]); i=2 removes B (last, pop to [A]); i=1 processes A. All visited. For middle removal: array [A,B,C], B removed at i=2: C swaps to index 1, pop to [A,C]. C was already visited at i=3. A visited at i=1. No skip.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided for GenesisPoolManager initialization

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario describes a pool being 'swapped into an index already passed' and thus not processed. However, in reverse iteration, the element swapped in comes from position `length-1` (the current last element), which was at a higher index and was already visited in a prior iteration. The swapped element lands at position `i-1` (the current position), which the loop has just finished processing. The loop then moves to `i-2`, correctly visiting the next unprocessed element. No element is ever skipped. Forward iteration would have this problem, but reverse iteration is a known safe pattern for swap-and-pop.
validation_result.impact_assessment: No impact. The code correctly handles the iteration-with-removal pattern. All live native tokens are processed exactly once during each call to checkBeforeEpochFlip and checkAtEpochFlip.
validation_result.replication_plan: Write a test with 5 live tokens where 3 need removal (indices 0, 2, 4). Call checkBeforeEpochFlip. Verify all 3 are set to NOT_QUALIFIED and removed from liveNativeTokens, while the other 2 are correctly transitioned. Assert final liveNativeTokens contains only the 2 non-removed tokens.
